posts.title: The Bangalore Job Seeker's Guide
posts.mobiledoc:
{"version": "0.3.1", "atoms": [], "cards": [["html", {"html": "

I moved to Bangalore with two suitcases, a laptop with a dying battery, and a spreadsheet of companies I'd never hear back from. Nobody hands you a map. This is the one I wish someone had handed me.

Who this is for

  • You're moving to Bangalore for work — or you're already here and stuck.
  • You're early-to-mid career in tech: engineering, data, design, marketing.
  • You want specifics, not "network more!" advice.

How to use it

Eight steps, in the order they actually happen. Walk it start to finish if you're still planning the move, or jump straight to the step you're stuck on — each one stands alone.

Everything here is what survived the edit. The parts that went badly are in too, because those are the useful parts.

The honest disclaimer

Numbers age. Rents move, salary bands drift, one metro line opens and a whole neighbourhood changes. Treat every figure as a signal, not a quote — and check the date at the top of the step.

"}]], "markups": [], "sections": [[10, 0]]}
posts.custom_excerpt: Moving to Bangalore for work? Eight steps, in the order they actually happen — runway, rent, resume, the loop, the offer, and the first thirty days. The map I wish someone had handed me.

posts.title: Step 1 — Before you book the ticket
posts.mobiledoc:
{"version": "0.3.1", "atoms": [], "cards": [["html", {"html": "

The worst version of this move is arriving with a one-way ticket, a hotel booked for four nights, and a plan that begins with "figure it out." Do these three first.

Work out your real runway

Take your savings, subtract the deposit (see step 2 — it is larger than you think), and divide what's left by your monthly burn. If the answer is under four months, you'll interview from a position of fear, and it shows.

  • Deposit — typically several months' rent, paid up front.
  • Setup — a mattress, a desk, a chair you won't hate by month three.
  • Float — the first salary can take six weeks to land.

Start the search before you land

The single highest-leverage thing: begin applying six to eight weeks before you move, with a local address already on the resume. Recruiters filter on location, and "relocating" reads as "will ghost us in round three."

Get the paper trail together

Payslips, offer letters, relieving letter, ID, degree certificate. Scan everything now, while you still have access to a printer and to whoever signs things at your current job.

The document you can't get is always the one from the employer you left on bad terms. Get it before you leave.
"}]], "markups": [], "sections": [[10, 0]]}
posts.custom_excerpt: The three things worth settling while you still have leverage and a return date.

posts.title: Step 2 — Where to live, and what it actually costs
posts.mobiledoc:
{"version": "0.3.1", "atoms": [], "cards": [["html", {"html": "

Bangalore is not one city. It's several, joined by traffic. Pick where you live by where you'll work, not by where the nice cafés are — the cafés are everywhere; the commute is forever.

The rough map

  • Koramangala / Indiranagar — startups, expensive, walkable, loud.
  • HSR Layout — the compromise everyone eventually makes.
  • Whitefield / Marathahalli — the big campuses. Cheaper, and far from everything else.
  • North (Hebbal / Yelahanka) — airport side, quieter, a different city entirely.

The deposit

This is the part that ambushes people. Deposits here run several months' rent — sometimes many. Budget for it as a hard number before you fall in love with a flat.

The commute test

Before signing anything: travel from that flat to that office, at 9am, on a weekday. Not on a map — actually do it. A 9km trip can be 20 minutes or 80. The map will lie to you; the Tuesday won't.

"}]], "markups": [], "sections": [[10, 0]]}
posts.custom_excerpt: Neighbourhoods decoded by commute, not by vibe. Plus the deposit nobody warns you about.

posts.title: Step 3 — Documents and the boring admin
posts.mobiledoc:
{"version": "0.3.1", "atoms": [], "cards": [["html", {"html": "

Nothing here is interesting, and all of it will block you at exactly the wrong moment.

The checklist

  • Government photo ID and address proof.
  • PAN — needed for the salary account and the offer.
  • Bank account you can actually operate locally.
  • Every payslip from the last year.
  • Relieving and experience letters from each previous employer.
  • Degree certificates — the physical ones, not a photo of a photo.

Background verification is real

Most mid-size and large employers run a third-party check. A gap you didn't mention, or a title that's inflated by one level, surfaces here — after you've resigned from the old job. Say the true thing on the form.

Nobody has ever lost an offer for a six-month gap they explained. People lose offers for six-month gaps they hid.
"}]], "markups": [], "sections": [[10, 0]]}
posts.custom_excerpt: The unglamorous checklist that blocks your offer, your flat, and your salary account.

posts.title: Step 4 — The resume Bangalore actually reads
posts.mobiledoc:
{"version": "0.3.1", "atoms": [], "cards": [["html", {"html": "

Your resume gets two readers: a parser, then a human with about nine seconds. Write for both, in that order.

The shape

  • One page under eight years' experience. Two is a flex you haven't earned yet.
  • Results, not responsibilities. "Owned the billing service" is a job description. "Cut billing failures 40% by rewriting retry logic" is a resume line.
  • Numbers wherever they're honest. Latency, revenue, users, hours saved.

The parser pass

Applicant tracking systems match on words. If the posting says Salesforce and your resume says CRM platform, you don't exist. Mirror the posting's vocabulary — where it's true.

Before: Responsible for CRM platform work across teams.
After:  Built Apex + Lightning Web Components on Salesforce;
        cut case-routing time 35% across 4 support teams.

The link that does the work

One link, working, at the top. A repo, a site, a reel. Not five. The one you'd defend in an interview.

"}]], "markups": [], "sections": [[10, 0]]}
posts.custom_excerpt: One page, results first, and the keyword pass you can't opt out of.

posts.title: Step 5 — Where the jobs actually get posted
posts.mobiledoc:
{"version": "0.3.1", "atoms": [], "cards": [["html", {"html": "

Volume is a trap. A hundred cold applications lose to four warm ones, every time. Ranked by what actually converted for me:

The ranking

  1. Someone who's met you. A former colleague forwarding your resume internally beats everything else combined.
  2. The company's own careers page. Posted earlier, read more carefully, fewer applicants.
  3. Recruiters who specialise in your stack. Not the ones who message "Dear Candidate."
  4. Job boards. High volume, low hit-rate. Use them to find companies, then apply direct.

Manufacturing the warm intro

You don't have a network here yet. You build one by being visibly useful in public: answer things, publish what you learn, show up to the meetup twice. Not "networking" — just being someone whose name a person can recall in a hiring meeting.

The job you get is usually from the person you helped eight months before you needed anything.
"}]], "markups": [], "sections": [[10, 0]]}
posts.custom_excerpt: The channel mix that works, ranked by hit-rate rather than by volume.

posts.title: Step 6 — The interview loop, decoded
posts.mobiledoc:
{"version": "0.3.1", "atoms": [], "cards": [["html", {"html": "

The loop looks like five conversations. It's really four different tests wearing similar clothes.

The rounds

  • Recruiter screen — testing: are your numbers and timeline real? Be boringly consistent.
  • Technical — testing: can you think out loud while uncomfortable? Narrate. Silence reads as stuck.
  • System / craft round — testing: do you know what you don't know? Say "I'd measure that" instead of guessing.
  • Hiring manager — testing: will this person make my life easier or harder?

The question that decides it

"Tell me about something that went wrong." Everyone has the story. Almost nobody has the second half: what you changed afterward so it couldn't happen again. That second half is the whole answer.

Your questions matter more than you think

Ask what the last person in this role struggled with. The pause before the answer tells you more than the answer does.

"}]], "markups": [], "sections": [[10, 0]]}
posts.custom_excerpt: What each round is really testing, and the one question that decides most of them.

posts.title: Step 7 — Negotiating the offer
posts.mobiledoc:
{"version": "0.3.1", "atoms": [], "cards": [["html", {"html": "

The offer is the highest-leverage ten minutes of the whole process, and most people rush through it out of relief.

CTC is not salary

Cost-to-company bundles things you can't spend: employer contributions, notional benefits, a bonus that's conditional, equity on a four-year cliff. Ask for the monthly in-hand number. Compare offers on that, and on equity separately.

How to ask

  • Never name the first number if you can help it. "What's the band for this level?"
  • Anchor on the market and the scope, not on your last salary.
  • Ask once, clearly, with a reason. Then stop talking.
"I'm excited about this. Based on the scope we discussed and
 what I'm seeing for this level, I was targeting X. Is there
 room to get there?"

Get it in writing

Verbal promises about a title bump "after six months" are not compensation. If it isn't in the letter, it doesn't exist.

"}]], "markups": [], "sections": [[10, 0]]}
posts.custom_excerpt: Bands, the CTC illusion, and how to ask for more without blowing it up.

posts.title: Step 8 — Your first 30 days
posts.mobiledoc:
{"version": "0.3.1", "atoms": [], "cards": [["html", {"html": "

You got the job. The goal now is not to impress anyone — it's to become someone people trust with the next thing.

Week one: shut up and map

Write down every system, every name, every acronym you don't know. Ask the dumb question in week one, when it's free. In month three it costs you.

Ship something small, early

Not the redesign. The tiny irritating thing everyone complains about and nobody owns. Fix that in your first fortnight and you buy credibility for the big thing later.

Draw the real org chart

There's the one on the wiki, and there's the one where three people actually decide things. Find the second one. Be useful to it.

And then?

That's the route. If it saved you a month of flailing, it did its job. If you hit a step I got wrong — and there will be one — tell me, and I'll fix it here.

Cut the chaos. Keep the part that survived the edit.
"}]], "markups": [], "sections": [[10, 0]]}
posts.custom_excerpt: How to land well: the ramp, the map, and the one thing to ship early.

tags.name: Bangalore Job Seeker Guide
tags.description: The field guide to landing a tech job in Bangalore.
